text: This video demonstrates our structured approach to excision of endometriosis, the SO-SHORE excision technique. This lady was very symptomatic and we knew already that she has a full thickness rectal nodule. We start with sigmoid mobilization. This helps us in left ureterolysis and also completely frees up the bowel then ovarian mobilization in this lady there was a small endometrioma it's very important that the ovaries are completely freed up because disease can hide itself just underneath we're doing the same with the right ovary both of the ovaries had a small endometrioma within them this was excised on both sides Then we do ovarian suspension.

The uterus is suspended, allowing clear access for the assistant. The surgeon uses cold scissors to separate the bowel from the back of the uterus, entering the rectovaginal space. Fat from the parorectal space becomes visible. Blunt and sharp dissection techniques are used to separate structures in the rectovaginal space. On the left side, ureterolysis is performed, revealing the left ureter.

The surgeon carefully avoids cutting the hypogastric nerves while entering the left pararectal space. The dissection proceeds to skeletonize important structures and remove abnormal tissue from the utero-sacral ligament and pararectal space. The right parietal space is opened, and the segmental bowel resection enters the holy plane behind the rectum, anterior to the hypogastric fascia, revealing a full thickness nodule.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the bowel, ensuring the operating field remains clean and tidy. The dissection continues along the tissue planes, despite challenges posed by obliterated planes due to deep infiltrative endometriosis. All visible endometriosis is meticulously excised, leaving no remnants.

The surgeon cleans the bowel and identifies the site for stapler application. Mr. Nisar removes the bowel segment and brings out the proximal end of the resected segment. The anvil is placed, and the anastomosis is performed to connect the bowel ends.
This procedure took two hours and twenty three minutes skin to skin and the patient did very well and went home two days post-operatively.
